subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
intro: Work the following ten problems in order; they are graded from direct separation through linear factors, initial value problems, the homogeneous substitution, and a full physical model. Solutions follow in Part III.

takeaway.title: Unit 2 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
Every method in this unit reduces to a single move --- rewrite the equation
into a form you can integrate. \highlight{Separation of variables}
splits a factored rate law; the \highlight{integrating factor
$\mu = e^{\int P\,dx}$} collapses a linear left side into one derivative; and
the \highlight{substitution $y = vx$} turns a homogeneous equation
separable. Read the structure first, and the technique always follows.

problems.solution:
Separate, then integrate.
\[ \work{y\,dy = x\,dx} \]
\[ \work{\frac{y^{2}}{2} = \frac{x^{2}}{2} + C_{1}} \]
\[ \work{y^{2} = x^{2} + C} \qquad\text{(general solution).} \]

Split the exponential as
$e^{x-y} = e^{x}e^{-y}$, then separate.
\[ \work{e^{\,y}\,dy = e^{\,x}\,dx} \]
\[ \work{e^{\,y} = e^{\,x} + C} \]
\[ \work{y = \ln\!\left(e^{\,x} + C\right)}. \]

problems.problem: Solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = y\cos x$, and identify any solution that is lost when you divide by $y$.
problems.solution:
Separate (assuming $y \neq 0$), integrate,
and exponentiate.
\[ \work{\frac{1}{y}\,dy = \cos x\,dx} \]
\[ \work{\ln|y| = \sin x + C} \]
\[ \work{y = C\,e^{\sin x}}. \]
Dividing by $y$ assumed $y \neq 0$, so it dropped the equilibrium solution
$\work{y = 0}$ --- which is recovered as the special case $C = 0$.

problems.problem: Solve the linear equation $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 3y = 6$ using an integrating factor.
problems.solution:
Here $P = 3$, so the integrating factor is
$\work{\mu = e^{\int 3\,dx} = e^{3x}}$. Multiply through:
\[ \work{\frac{d}{dx}\big[e^{3x}y\big] = 6\,e^{3x}} \]
\[ \work{e^{3x}y = \int 6\,e^{3x}\,dx = 2\,e^{3x} + C} \]
\[ \work{y = 2 + C\,e^{-3x}}. \]

problems.problem: Solve $x\dfrac{dy}{dx} + y = x^{2}$ by first writing it in standard linear form.
problems.solution:
Divide by $x$ to reach standard form:
\[ \work{\frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{1}{x}\,y = x}, \qquad
   \work{\mu = e^{\int (1/x)\,dx} = e^{\ln|x|} = x}. \]
Multiply through and integrate:
\[ \work{\frac{d}{dx}\big[xy\big] = x\cdot x = x^{2}} \]
\[ \work{xy = \frac{x^{3}}{3} + C} \]
\[ \work{y = \frac{x^{2}}{3} + \frac{C}{x}}. \]

With $P = \frac{2}{x}$, integrate the
coefficient and exponentiate:
\[ \work{\mu = e^{\int (2/x)\,dx} = e^{2\ln|x|} = x^{2}}. \]
Multiply through and integrate:
\[ \work{\frac{d}{dx}\big[x^{2}y\big] = x^{2}\cdot x = x^{3}} \]
\[ \work{x^{2}y = \frac{x^{4}}{4} + C} \]
\[ \work{y = \frac{x^{2}}{4} + \frac{C}{x^{2}}}. \]

problems.problem: Solve the initial value problem $\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 2y = 4$ with $y(0) = 1$.
problems.solution:
The integrating factor is
$\work{\mu = e^{\int 2\,dx} = e^{2x}}$. Multiply through and
integrate:
\[ \work{\frac{d}{dx}\big[e^{2x}y\big] = 4\,e^{2x}} \]
\[ \work{e^{2x}y = 2\,e^{2x} + C} \]
\[ \work{y = 2 + C\,e^{-2x}}. \]
Apply the initial condition $y(0) = 1$:
\[ \work{1 = 2 + C \;\Longrightarrow\; C = -1} \]
\[ \work{y = 2 - e^{-2x}}. \]

problems.problem: Solve the homogeneous equation $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{x + y}{x}$ using the substitution $y = vx$.
problems.solution:
Rewrite as $\frac{dy}{dx} = 1 + \frac{y}{x}$
and substitute $y = vx$, so $\frac{dy}{dx} = v + x\frac{dv}{dx}$:
\[ \work{v + x\frac{dv}{dx} = 1 + v} \]
\[ \work{x\frac{dv}{dx} = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; dv = \frac{1}{x}\,dx} \]
\[ \work{v = \ln|x| + C}. \]
Back-substitute $v = \frac{y}{x}$:
\[ \work{y = x\ln|x| + Cx}. \]

problems.problem: Solve the homogeneous equation $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{x^{2} + y^{2}}{xy}$ using $y = vx$.
problems.solution:
Substitute $y = vx$. The right side becomes
\[ \work{\frac{x^{2} + v^{2}x^{2}}{x\cdot vx} = \frac{1 + v^{2}}{v}}. \]
With $\frac{dy}{dx} = v + x\frac{dv}{dx}$,
\[ \work{v + x\frac{dv}{dx} = \frac{1 + v^{2}}{v} = \frac{1}{v} + v} \]
\[ \work{x\frac{dv}{dx} = \frac{1}{v} \;\Longrightarrow\; v\,dv = \frac{1}{x}\,dx} \]
\[ \work{\frac{v^{2}}{2} = \ln|x| + C_{1} \;\Longrightarrow\; v^{2} = 2\ln|x| + C}. \]
Back-substitute $v = \frac{y}{x}$:
\[ \work{\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}} = 2\ln|x| + C \;\Longrightarrow\; y^{2} = x^{2}\big(2\ln|x| + C\big)}. \]

problems.problem: \strong{(Application)} An object at $100^{\circ}$ is placed in a room held at $20^{\circ}$. After $10$ minutes its temperature is $60^{\circ}$. Find the temperature function $T(t)$ and determine how long it takes the object to reach $30^{\circ}$.
problems.solution:
Newton's law of cooling with surrounding
temperature $T_{s} = 20$ and initial temperature $T_{0} = 100$ gives
\[ \work{T(t) = 20 + (100 - 20)\,e^{-kt} = 20 + 80\,e^{-kt}}. \]
Use the reading $T(10) = 60$ to find $k$:
\[ \work{60 = 20 + 80\,e^{-10k} \;\Longrightarrow\; 80\,e^{-10k} = 40} \]
\[ \work{e^{-10k} = \tfrac{1}{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; -10k = -\ln 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; k = \frac{\ln 2}{10}}. \]
Hence the temperature function is
\[ \work{T(t) = 20 + 80\,e^{-(\ln 2 / 10)\,t} = 20 + 80\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t/10}}. \]
To reach $30^{\circ}$, solve $T(t) = 30$:
\[ \work{30 = 20 + 80\,e^{-kt} \;\Longrightarrow\; 80\,e^{-kt} = 10 \;\Longrightarrow\; e^{-kt} = \tfrac{1}{8}} \]
\[ \work{-kt = -\ln 8 = -3\ln 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; t = \frac{3\ln 2}{k} = \frac{3\ln 2}{\ln 2 / 10} = 30 \text{ minutes}}. \]
